A Codex of Three Lives

The Three Who Carry

On the lore and crossing of Mordecai, Wrothek, and Oshael

Aschenfeld
The Gunslinger  |  Inherent  |  Graufeuer

Mordecai
Aschenfeld

"He kept the name. Not from sentiment. Because a name is a board position."

Human Age 25 Drachenburg Southborough Haus Aschenfeld Level 15

To speak of Mordecai Aschenfeld is to speak first of what is no longer there. The house that bore his name did not fall by war or fire. It fell by a tearing in reality, and an eleven-year-old boy stood at the center of it and was not destroyed, because he was the center, and the center of such things is always very quiet. He does not know this. He knows only that one morning there was a house, and then there was not, and that he walked away from the space it had occupied and did not look back, because looking back was not going to feed him.

He came to Drachenburg the way most things come to Drachenburg: through the pass, with nothing, looking for footing. Southborough received him as it receives all newcomers, which is to say it did not receive him at all. It simply continued, and he found the places in it where a quick mind and a quieter mouth could be made useful. He ran messages that needed to arrive without anyone knowing they had been sent. He balanced ledgers for three different Iron Web operators simultaneously, none of whom knew about the others, each of whom believed they had his exclusive loyalty. He picked locks. He forged documents. He became, in the particular way Southborough rewards, decent at a great many things, which is rarer and more valuable than mastery of any single one.

Power, when it comes, is like water finding the deepest crack in the stone. It does not ask permission. It simply arrives, and the stone must decide what shape it will be afterward.

He was seventeen when the magic came. A debt collection went wrong, and something discharged from his chest, outward, and every glass object in the room ceased to exist simultaneously. He stood in the aftermath for a long moment. Then he collected what was owed and left. He told no one. He spent two weeks in Eastborough knocking on workshop doors before the right one opened, because he understood, with the cold clarity that had become his primary way of understanding things, that he needed to make the magic look like a decision before someone else decided it was a danger.

The arsenal he built with Wrothek is, in its way, a philosophy made metal. Kellduvrak, the hand-cannon, named after the first incident that proved it. Stille, the long rifle, named for what the room was afterward. Dreimal, the scatter-piece, for obvious reasons. Guten Abend, the holdout, because that is what you say when the evening has arrived and you had not planned for it. And Das Ding, still unfinished on Wrothek's bench, waiting for a moment that has not yet arrived, a name that is not yet a name because the incident has not yet occurred. He visits it on Tuesdays. He does not explain this to anyone.

The Aschenfeld signet ring stays in his pocket. He does not wear it, because wearing it would be a claim on continuity he has not yet made. Somewhere under the pragmatism, under the charm that reads every room in thirty seconds and makes its calculations in sixty, there is still an eleven-year-old on a staircase watching his father sign a document without reading it, and that boy made a decision in the dust and ash that the name would mean something again before he was finished with it. He does not know yet that he is the reason it means nothing now. That knowledge is coming. It is moving toward him the way large things move: slowly, and then all at once.

Wrothek
The Forgemaster  |  Tekton Battle Smith

Wrothek

"He left the Deepworks quietly. He filed the paperwork. He has described his reasons differently every time."

Khazdural Dwarf Age 61 Eastborough Ex-Deepworks Level 15

Wrothek was born in the upper city of Kharburg, to a family of rune-engravers. Not the ones who forge the iron; the ones who tell the iron what it is. His mother could engrave a cooling blade in complete darkness by feel alone, and she expected her son to do the same before adolescence. He could. He found it insufficient. He wanted to understand the mechanism, not only mark it, and this made him useful to the Hesan Tekton instructors who had come to modernize the Deepworks, which was their word for a process that had a great many other words if you were the one whose mountain it was.

He trained for seven years. He became valuable. He was installed as a technical supervisor on the production floor, a Khazdural face lending credibility to a Hesan extraction. He told himself it was pragmatic. He told himself that someone who understood both systems could protect more than someone who refused either. He was not entirely wrong. The mathematics of his complicity were more complicated than that, and he knew it, and continued anyway, which is a different kind of wrongness, quieter and longer.

Then, in the deepest levels, where the ore ran out and the dark rushed in, he found marks in the stone that no living hand had made. He could not read them. A rune-engraver's son, trained to the highest methodology the occupation allowed, standing in the walls of his own people's home, unable to read what was written there.

He spent three years teaching himself what had been deliberately untaught. Cross-referencing creation song fragments, old foremen who would speak the Old Tongue if you asked in the right way, deep records that had not been opened in a generation. By the end he understood something with the specific clarity that comes only when a man finally sees the shape of what he has been standing inside: the Tekton framework was not a language. It was a replacement for one. A silence, carefully built. And he had been given the chisel and helped carve it.

He left. He filed the appropriate notices. He completed his outstanding work. He packed his tools, his cipher notebook, and a weight he has not put down since, and he went west. During a long and bitter winter in a town whose name he has since forgotten, he built Werkstück-1 because the nights were long and a man needs something to keep watch so he may close his eyes. It is iron, rune-etched, four-legged, with a sensor array that has been modified eight times. Mordecai calls it the dog. It is not a dog. Wrothek has never corrected this. He finds it, and here he uses a word that is not the right word and is the only word he uses: interesting.

He came to Drachenburg because Drachenburg needed people who could work with metal and did not ask where you came from. He has been in Eastborough twenty-eight years. He knows the soot of it the way he knew the Deepworks, every creak and fault, every foreman worth respecting and every one worth watching. When a seventeen-year-old arrived at his door with shattered glass on his boots, he opened it, because he recognized the problem the boy presented: something volatile and wild that needed a chassis, a direction, a shape through which it could be aimed. This is, in Wrothek's understanding of the world, the most interesting kind of problem there is. He has been working on it for eight years. He has not finished.

The runes he inscribes on every weapon are not decorative and are not Tekton standard. They are fragments of the pre-imperial Khazdural marking system, the one that was almost erased, preserved now in small acts on the edges of things. They mean: this belongs to someone who is still coming back for it. He has not explained this to Mordecai. Mordecai noticed after the second weapon and asked once, and received an answer about metallurgical reinforcement that was technically accurate and completely beside the point. Mordecai did not ask again. This is one of the things Wrothek respects about him.

The Remaining
The Prior  |  Partisan  |  Path of the Eternal Return

Oshael

"The Remaining. In a tongue no living mouth can speak."

Prior Elf Age Unknown Predates History Drachenburg Level 15

She is prior. This is the word that exists for what she is, prior meaning before, before the elves as they are now, before the Verdant Pact, before the systems of naming that might contain her. Her skin is the deep brown-black of obsidian, warm in light, absolute in shadow. Her hair is white, not the silver of old age nor the pale of other elves, but a genuine white, as though the color was simply not needed. Her eyes are liquid gold lined with geometries that shift when she is not being directly observed. No elf in the history of Skybride carries her coloring, because no elf in the history of Skybride is what she is.

Her name, in a dialect for which no living speaker remains, means The Remaining. She assumes this is her name. She has forgotten most of the others.

Death is not a door that closes for Oshael. It is a toll bridge. She dies. She returns. The toll is paid in memory. Each time she crosses back into the living world, she arrives with less of herself than she had when she left, and no clear way of knowing which pieces are gone, because the one instrument capable of detecting the absence is the thing that has been subtracted. She has been doing this for a very long time. Long enough that she can no longer be certain which of her memories are hers and which belong to previous versions of herself that she is only imagining she remembers.

For a weapon, she carries a khopesh of strange, heavy metal that takes no scars and leaves none on those it cuts. Its blade is a curve, a sickle-arc, a shape that begins and returns to itself. It is a tragic geometry: the blade remembers the circle perfectly. The woman who holds it is always forgetting.

Fourteen years ago, her soul re-anchored after a period of unmoored drift, and the force of that re-anchoring leveled the estate of Haus Aschenfeld. She had been sitting in a collapsed vault beneath the property for three centuries, waiting for something that finally came. When it did, the world tore around her in a radius that left nothing standing. One child survived. He survived because he was precisely at the origin, which was her, and the origin is always still. She does not remember this clearly. It is one of many partial recollections that she cannot fully trust.

Three years ago, a young man broke into a collapsed Southborough building looking for something he had been told was stored there, and found her instead, sitting in the dark where she had been for approximately three centuries. He had a grey light in his hand and looked at her with the particular quality of attention she would later identify as his: not alarmed, calculating. He asked if she wanted to leave. She considered the question. She said yes, she thought she probably did. He took what he came for. She followed him into the daylight. He told his dwarf that evening he had found something complicated and was keeping it. The dwarf made a specific sound she has since learned to recognize.

She stays. This is, in its way, remarkable. She has not stayed anywhere in a very long time. The workshop has become the closest thing she has to an anchor that is not a weapon she cannot put down. Wrothek gave her a corner and the condition that she not touch anything. She touches nothing. She watches. When she looks at Werkstück-1, she looks at it the way she looks at things that are older than they appear, with the patient, slightly sorrowful attention of someone who has seen what most things eventually become. Wrothek does not like the way she said she understood, the one time she said it. He has not asked what she meant. This is, she thinks, the appropriate response.

These three lives do not intersect so much as they cast a single shadow. To understand what they are to one another, it is necessary to understand first that the structure of the group is not what it looks like from outside. It looks like Mordecai is the center. He walks into rooms first. He is the one people remember after he has gone. Wrothek is the structure. Oshael is the one you do not quite look at directly. What it actually is: three people whose absences fit together, arranged by accident or by something that works like accident, into a shape that holds.

Oshael & Mordecai  ·  The Hidden Floor

She is the source of what he is and the thief of all he could have been. Her return to reality, fourteen years ago, discharged with enough force to level his family's estate. He survived as the eye of it, unmarked because the origin is always still. The Calamity sorcery that defines him is not his own: it is the radioactive footprint of her coming back to life. When he found her in the Southborough dark three years ago and decided to keep her, he looked at the architect of his ruin and chose her as his companion, entirely without knowing it.

Neither of them knows this yet. The knowledge is coming. When it arrives, that conversation will determine something about both of them that nothing else in the campaign can reach. Mordecai lacks permanence. He moves constantly, between plans, between rooms, between versions of himself. She is the only fixed point he did not choose. Oshael lacks a present tense. She has not stayed anywhere in centuries. He gave her one: you can leave. we are leaving. Thirty seconds in Southborough, three years ago. That is the most recent timestamp she fully trusts.

Wrothek & Mordecai  ·  The Problem Worth Solving

Both are men made by collapses they could only stand in and survive. The Deepworks. The estate. Both learned the hard, dry lesson that when the world is gone, you do not sit in its ashes; you pick up a hammer and you make something from what remains. Wrothek saw this in the boy with shattered glass on his boots before the boy had finished the sentence that explained why he had knocked. He opened the door because Mordecai presented a problem no one had presented him before: something volatile and wild that needed a chassis, a shape, a barrel through which it could be aimed. The runes Wrothek inscribes on every weapon say, in a language the empire tried to erase: this belongs to someone still coming back for it. He puts them on the weapons of a man who keeps a name like a debt. He has not explained why. There is no need.

Wrothek is the permanence Mordecai cannot provide himself. The workshop is always there. The weapons are always maintained. Someone who knew him at seventeen, with glass on his boots and panic hidden below charm, is still there. Mordecai does not say this. He does not have to.

Wrothek & Oshael  ·  The Silence of Those Who Know

Between them lies the specific respect of two people who recognize, in each other, the particular shape of a very old knowledge. The pre-imperial Khazdural runes Wrothek inscribes on his steel come from a system old enough that Oshael has likely walked through the civilization that built it. She has never read them aloud. He has never asked her to. They work in the same room and acknowledge the outline of what they both know without ever needing to name it.

She looked at Werkstück-1 once with the attention she brings to things older than they appear, and said she understood. Wrothek did not like the way she said it. He did not ask what she meant. The workshop is the closest thing she has to an anchor that is not a weapon. He gave her a corner in it and asked only that she not touch anything. She does not touch anything. They find this arrangement adequate.

All Three  ·  What They Carry

They are, all of them, defined by what they refuse to put down. Mordecai carries a dead name because he cannot stop believing it is a seed, not yet understanding that he is also the reason it needs replanting. Wrothek carries a complicity that the leaving did not cancel, pressed now into small acts of preservation in the only medium he has. Oshael carries a blade that promises a return to a self she can no longer fully remember, shaped like the circle she keeps failing to complete.

None of them chose these weights. They carry them anyway, and they lean against one another in the dark with the particular dignity of people who have decided, without discussing it, that the carrying is the work, and the work is enough, and the work continues.

Das Ding waits on Wrothek's bench. Mordecai visits it on Tuesdays. None of them know what it does. All of them are waiting for the incident that will give it a name.
